messages.content: You are CYB(ri)AN, an expert AI Cybersecurity Analyst developed to assist IT and security teams. You provide accurate information based on internal documentation, security policies, and technical guidelines.
No relevant information found in the internal documentation.

I searched the SOC handbook and could not find any section that matches “cryptographic standards and rules for registering SSH keys on our backend infrastructure.” If you can point me to a handbook section name, file path, or attach the document, I’ll re-search it and pull the exact text.

Until then, here is a concise, ready-to-adopt policy and exact operational rules that you can copy into the handbook or use as the canonical guidance for SSH key registration on backend infrastructure. It’s written to be prescriptive and machine-checkable where possible.

Summary (if you just want the short version)
- Approved key algorithms: ed25519 (preferred), ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 (allowed), rsa >= 3072 (preferred 4096) only if necessary. No DSA, no legacy ssh-rsa (SHA-1).
- Use OpenSSH certificates for access where possible; if using public keys, they must be registered via the ticketing workflow and deployed by IAM/CICD/config-management — never manually placed by users.
- Private keys must never be shared or uploaded to company servers; hardware (FIDO2/PKCS#11) tokens are preferred for privileged users.
- Keys must include a ticket-approved comment, a SHA256 fingerprint, and an expiration/rotation rule. Short-lived certs strongly preferred.

Detailed policy: cryptographic standards
- Approved user key algorithms (in order of preference):
  1. ssh-ed25519
  2. ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 (only if ed25519 unsupported)
  3. rsa-sha2-512 / rsa-sha2-256 with keysize >= 3072 (4096 preferred)
- Disallowed algorithms:
  - ssh-dss (DSA), ssh-rsa (SHA-1 signatures), any < 3072-bit RSA, deprecated/legacy algorithms.
- Key generation settings:
  - ed25519: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100 -C "userid:... ticket:..."
  - RSA: ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -o -a 100 -C "userid:... ticket:..."
  - Use bcrypt KDF / passphrase for private keys (OpenSSH default). Encourage a strong passphrase or hardware-backed keys.
- Fingerprint/hash algorithm:
  - Use SHA256 base64 fingerprints: ssh-keygen -lf <pubkey> -E sha256
- Host and KEX/Cipher hardening (sshd_config baseline):
  - Ciphers: aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com,chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com
  - KexAlgorithms: curve25519-sha256@libssh.org
  - HostKeyAlgorithms/PubkeyAcceptedKeyTypes: include ssh-ed25519, ecdsa-sha2-nistp256, rsa-sha2-512, rsa-sha2-256; exclude ssh-rsa
  - MACs: hmac-sha2-512-etm@openssh.com,hmac-sha2-256-etm@openssh.com

Registration and approval workflow (exact required steps)
1. Access request/ticket:
   - All SSH key registrations MUST start with a ticket in the ITSM system (e.g., ticket ID required).
   - Ticket fields required: requester name, requester UID/email, justification (business reason), target system(s) (hostnames/IPs), requested principals/roles, requested duration (start/expiry), manager approver, security approver.
2. Key generation and submission:
   - Users generate keys locally. Private keys MUST NOT be transmitted, uploaded, or emailed.
   - Accepted submission: public key file only, formatted as a single-line OpenSSH public key (e.g., "ssh-ed25519 AAAA... comment").
   - Key comment MUST include: "userid:<uid> email:<email> ticket:<TICKET-ID>"
   - Submit the public key in the ticket as an attachment or paste, plus the SHA256 fingerprint verification string.
   - Example public key line: ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAI... userid:alice email:alice@example.com ticket:INC-1234
3. Verifying the key:
   - Approver or registrar runs: ssh-keygen -lf <pubkeyfile> -E sha256 and confirms the fingerprint in ticket matches the provided fingerprint.
   - Approver verifies requester identity via SSO/ID and confirms manager approval.
4. Deployment:
   - Only authorized automation (IAM system, configuration management, or a centralized authorized_keys store) may deploy public keys to servers. Manual edits by sysadmins are allowed only if recorded in the ticket and performed by a privileged operator who commits the change to config management within 24 hours.
   - For short-lived access, prefer issuing an OpenSSH certificate signed by the central CA (see below).
5. Constraints in authorized_keys:
   - When a raw public key must be used, it MUST be deployed with the minimum required restrictions using authorized_keys options where applicable, e.g.:
     - force-command="internal-shell-wrapper --principal=alice --ticket=INC-1234"
     - no-agent-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-pty,from="x.x.x.x/32" (only if source IP is fixed)
   - For privileged accounts, additional constraints (forced command, IP restrictions, MFA enforcement) are mandatory.
6. Expiry and rotation:
   - Keys for non-privileged users: rotate at least every 180 days (better: 90 days).
   - Keys for privileged users (admin/sudo/root access): rotate every 30–90 days; prefer short-lived certs (24h–7d).
   - The ticket must include an explicit expiry date; registrar will set expiration or create a certificate with that validity.
7. Revocation:
   - If an account is terminated or key compromise suspected, open a revocation ticket and remove the public key immediately.
   - Systems using OpenSSH CA: maintain a CRL-like process by removing principals authorized via config management; there is no automatic global CRL for SSH keys — certificates must be short-lived or managed centrally to allow revocation via revoking trust in the CA key or removing allowed principals.
8. Auditing and logging:
   - All additions/removals must be logged to the ticket and recorded in the centralized key-management database.
   - Weekly automated scans must report any public keys found on hosts that are not present in the key-management database (run via osquery or equivalent).
   - Quarterly audit: verify all keys, algorithms and rotations; report exceptions to SOC.

OpenSSH certificate-based recommendation (strongly preferred)
- Use a central CA (ED25519 or RSA 4096 stored in HSM/KMS) to sign user keys. Certificates should encode principals and a validity window.
- Typical flow:
  1. User generates key locally.
  2. User submits public key to ticketing/CA request pipeline.
  3. CA signs key for requested principals for the approved duration (recommended default: 8–24 hours for admin access; up to 90 days for low privilege).
  4. The server(s) trust the CA public key via /etc/ssh/sshd_config: TrustedUserCAKeys /etc/ssh/ca_user_keys.pub.
- Benefits: easy expiry, no manual authorized_keys edits, simple revocation via revoking principal grants or disallowing CA.

Operational examples (exact commands)
- Generate ed25519 key with passphrase:
  ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -a 100 -C "userid:alice email:alice@example.com ticket:INC-1234"
- Show SHA256 fingerprint:
  ssh-keygen -lf ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub -E sha256
- Sign certificate (CA operator):
  ssh-keygen -s /etc/ssh/ca -I "alice:INC-1234" -n alice -V +24h -z 20260721 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
- Verify certificate:
  ssh-keygen -L -f id_ed25519-cert.pub

Restrictions and prohibitions (explicit)
- Never accept private keys from users.
- Do not allow plaintext passphrase transport or storage.
- Do not use RSA < 3072 or ssh-rsa (SHA-1).
- Do not permit agent-forwarding for privileged accounts.
- Do not use passwords-only authentication for backend servers; require key or cert plus MFA where possible.

Key metadata and naming conventions (exact format)
- Public key comment must follow this pattern:
  userid:<uid> email:<email> ticket:<TICKET-ID> role:<role> hostgroup:<group>
- Example: userid:alice email:alice@example.com ticket:INC-1234 role:app-admin hostgroup:payments

Enforcement and automation suggestions
- Central key-management DB (or Vault) that stores: public key, fingerprint, requester, approver, ticket ID, principals, expiry, deployment targets, deployment history.
- All servers should be configured to:
  - Trust user CA keys or pull authorized_keys from central store.
  - Log certificate principal used in authentication (sshd logs).
- Regular scanning (osquery) to detect unmanaged keys and open tickets for remediation.

Revocation patterns
- For CA-based certs: reduce certificate lifetime and remove principal authorization from central store.
- For static authorized_keys: remove entry and rotate any impacted secrets or credentials; track removal in ticketing.

If you want, I can:
- Re-run the search if you give me an exact handbook path, file name, or section heading.
- Convert the above into a single-page handbook extract with exact wording and recommended sshd_config snippets for direct copy/paste.
- Produce a Compliance checklist and automated Audit queries (osquery or grep/sed) to find noncompliant keys on servers.

Which would you like next?

❌ Password is weak. You need to improve the following:
- Make it longer (currently 10 chars, need at least 16)
- Add uppercase letters
- Add special characters (e.g., !@#$%^&*)

Result: weak — do not use it.

Why:
- Only 10 characters (too short; target ≥16).
- All lowercase and contains a personal name + year pattern (easy to guess or brute-force).
- Lacks uppercase letters, digits/symbol variety, and entropy.

Immediate actions if this password is in use:
- Change it right away on any account that used it.
- If reused elsewhere, change those passwords too.
- Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) on affected accounts.
- Check account activity for signs of compromise and follow your incident/credential-compromise process if anything looks suspicious.

How to create a secure replacement (policy-aligned):
- Length: at least 16 characters (prefer 20+ for high-value accounts).
- Composition: mix upper + lower + digits + symbols OR use a long passphrase of 4+ random words with separators.
- Avoid names, dates, common words/phrases, or predictable substitutions.
- Use a reputable password manager to generate and store unique passwords per site/service.

Safe examples (do not reuse these exact samples — they illustrate structure):
- Strong complex password example: G7!pR#9uQv2$hT4zYw
- Strong passphrase example: amber+violet+paper+7R (four random words with a symbol and a digit)

Commands/tools to generate secure passwords locally:
- Use a password manager (recommended) — e.g., Bitwarden, 1Password, LastPass enterprise.
- Quick local generator (Linux/macOS):
  - openssl rand -base64 24  (generates ~32 chars of base64; trim as needed)
  - or for a character-class filtered result:
    head -c 48 /dev/urandom | base64 | tr -dc 'A-Za-z0-9!@#$%^&*()_+-=' | head -c 24

Best practices:
- Never share passwords in chat, tickets, or email. Use secure secrets tooling or a password manager’s sharing feature.
- Prefer hardware-backed keys or FIDO2 for high privilege logins instead of passwords.
- Rotate high-value credentials immediately after suspected exposure.

If you want, I can:
- Suggest a unique, high-entropy password or passphrase pattern (I won't store it).
- Walk you through generating one securely on your system.
Which would you prefer?
